bytes.topic: Gas & Fees — gas limit, base fee, priority fee (EIP-1559), gas optimization
bytes.title: Ethereum Gas & EIP-1559 — Fees You Control vs. Fees You Don't
bytes.body: Every Ethereum transaction consumes gas — a unit measuring computational work. Post-EIP-1559, fees split into two components: the `baseFee` (burned, protocol-set, fluctuates per block based on utilization) and the `maxPriorityFeePerGas` (your tip to the validator). You set `maxFeePerGas` as a ceiling — actual cost is `baseFee + priorityFee`, never exceeding your max. The `gasLimit` caps how much gas a transaction may consume; unused gas is refunded, but if execution exceeds the limit, the tx reverts and you still pay for work done. Use `eth_feeHistory` to sample recent base fees and build dynamic tip estimates instead of hardcoding. For contracts, reduce gas via tight packing of storage slots (`uint128` pairs share one slot), preferring `calldata` over `memory` for read-only function args, and caching `SLOAD` results in local variables — each cold `SLOAD` costs 2,100 gas vs. 100 for warm. `block.basefee` is accessible in Solidity since 0.8.7 for on-chain fee-aware logic.

bytes.topic: EVM Architecture — opcodes, gas model, stack-based execution, memory vs storage vs calldata
bytes.title: Ethereum EVM Architecture — Opcodes, Gas, and the Data Location Hierarchy
bytes.body:
The EVM is a stack-based virtual machine with a 256-bit word size. Every opcode pops and pushes values onto a max-depth-1024 stack — PUSH1 0x60 costs 3 gas and places one item; ADD pops two and pushes one for another 3 gas. Gas is deterministic: each opcode has a fixed base cost in the Yellow Paper, with dynamic surcharges for state access (SLOAD: 2100 gas cold, 100 warm via EIP-2929).

Data locations map to cost tiers. `calldata` is cheapest — read-only, passed with the transaction, costs 4 gas per zero byte and 16 per non-zero (EIP-2028). `memory` is byte-addressable, zero-initialized, and expands quadratically: cheap for small buffers, expensive past ~700 bytes. `storage` is a 32-byte key/value trie on disk — SSTORE to a cold slot costs 22,100 gas; a dirty re-write drops to 100. Misidentifying data locations is the most common gas bug in Solidity. Marking a function parameter `storage` instead of `calldata` in a `view` function wastes thousands of gas per call.

bytes.topic: Ethereum — accounts, transactions, block structure, consensus (PoS post-merge)
bytes.title: Ethereum Internals — Accounts, Transactions, and Post-Merge PoS in One Pass
bytes.body:
Ethereum has two account types: Externally Owned Accounts (EOAs), controlled by a private key with no code, and Contract Accounts, which hold EVM bytecode and storage. Both share the same state model: nonce, balance, storageRoot, codeHash — all committed to the world state trie.

A transaction from an EOA carries: nonce (replay protection), maxFeePerGas + maxPriorityFeePerGas (EIP-1559), gas limit, to, value, and data. The base fee is burned; only the priority fee goes to the validator. Access lists (EIP-2930) let you pre-warm storage slots, reducing gas for known reads.

Post-Merge, Ethereum's execution layer (EL) and consensus layer (CL) run as separate clients. The Engine API connects them — the CL drives block production via `engine_forkchoiceUpdatedV3`, and the EL executes and validates payloads. Validators stake 32 ETH, are pseudo-randomly assigned to committees, and attest to the head of the chain each epoch (32 slots × 12s). Finality arrives after two justified checkpoints via Casper FFG. There are no miners — `DIFFICULTY` opcode now returns `PREVRANDAO`, a VRF seed from the beacon chain.

bytes.topic: Layer 2 Scaling — why L1 is expensive, rollup approach, optimistic vs ZK
bytes.title: Ethereum Layer 2 Scaling — Rollups, Optimistic vs ZK, and Why L1 Fees Hurt
bytes.body:
Ethereum L1 executes every transaction on every full node — security through redundancy, but throughput caps at ~15 TPS and gas spikes during congestion. A single Uniswap v3 swap costs 100k–150k gas; at 30 gwei that's ~$4–12 depending on ETH price.

Rollups fix this by executing transactions off-chain and posting compressed calldata (or blobs via EIP-4844) to L1. L1 only verifies a proof or fraud window — not every opcode.

Optimistic rollups (Optimism, Arbitrum) assume transactions are valid and post state roots. A 7-day challenge window lets anyone submit a fraud proof using `eth_getProof` against the L1 state root. Cheap to post, expensive to dispute.

ZK rollups (zkSync Era, Starknet, Polygon zkEVM) generate a validity proof (SNARK/STARK) for every batch. L1 verifies the proof via a `verifier` contract — no trust, no waiting. Proving is compute-heavy but verification is O(1) gas.

EIP-4844 (Dencun upgrade) introduced blob-carrying transactions (`type 0x03`), giving rollups a dedicated ~128 KB/block data lane at ~10–100x cheaper than calldata, cutting L2 fees dramatically.

bytes.topic: Optimistic Rollups — fraud proofs, challenge period, withdrawal delay (Arbitrum, Optimism)
bytes.title: Ethereum Optimistic Rollups — Fraud Proofs, Challenge Windows, and Why Withdrawals Take 7 Days
bytes.body:
Optimistic rollups (Arbitrum, Optimism) post transaction batches to Ethereum L1 as calldata without on-chain execution — they assume validity by default. Security comes from fraud proofs: a 7-day challenge window during which any verifier can submit a fraud proof if the sequencer posted an invalid state root.

On Arbitrum, fraud proofs are interactive (multi-round bisection via the `RollupCore` contract), narrowing the dispute to a single L2 instruction executed on L1. Optimism's fault proof system (Cannon, live on mainnet since mid-2024) uses a MIPS-based single-round proof.

The 7-day withdrawal delay is a direct consequence — you must wait for the challenge window to close before `finalizeWithdrawalTransaction()` on L1 can be called. Bridges like Hop and Across exploit liquidity providers to give instant withdrawals, charging a fee in exchange for absorbing the delay risk.

For dApps, never assume L2 state is L1-finalized until the window closes. Use `portal.provenWithdrawals()` on the `OptimismPortal` contract to check withdrawal status programmatically.

bytes.topic: Transaction Lifecycle — mempool, ordering, finality, reorg risk
bytes.title: Ethereum Transaction Lifecycle — Mempool, Ordering, Finality, and Reorg Risk
bytes.body:
When you call eth_sendRawTransaction, your signed tx enters the public mempool (txpool). Validators running MEV-Boost pull txs via the builder API — block builders order them to maximize extractable value, not just by gas price. Priority fee (EIP-1559) still influences inclusion, but MEV searchers can sandwich or frontrun you regardless.

Once included in a block, the tx has 1 confirmation. Ethereum's LMD-GHOST + Casper FFG consensus finalizes checkpoints every 2 epochs (~12.8 min). Before finality, a reorg is possible: if a competing fork has more attestation weight, your block can be orphaned and the tx dropped back to the mempool — or lost entirely if the replacement block doesn't include it.

For high-value ops, poll eth_getTransactionReceipt and check block.finalized via eth_getBlockByNumber with tag "finalized". Never treat 1–2 confirmations as safe for irreversible actions. Use Flashbots Protect RPC to bypass the public mempool and reduce frontrun/reorg exposure on sensitive txs.

bytes.topic: Polygon — PoS chain, AggLayer, zkEVM, bridging assets
bytes.title: Ethereum + Polygon — PoS, zkEVM, AggLayer, and Bridging Assets
bytes.body: Polygon offers two distinct Ethereum-connected chains: Polygon PoS (a commit-chain using plasma checkpoints + Heimdall validators) and Polygon zkEVM (a Type-2 ZK-equivalent rollup posting validity proofs to Ethereum L1). They are not interchangeable. PoS bridges assets via the FxPortal or the native PoS bridge — locking ERC-20s on Ethereum and minting mapped tokens on PoS. zkEVM uses a canonical bridge where withdrawals require a ZK proof to be verified on L1 before funds unlock, adding a ~30–60min finality window versus PoS's ~7-day challenge period on plasma exits (though PoS MATIC withdrawals via the PoS bridge settle in ~3 hours). AggLayer is Polygon's unified liquidity protocol: chains publish ZK proofs to a single aggregator contract on Ethereum L1, enabling atomic cross-chain transactions without wrapping assets per hop. For production bridging on PoS, use the official `@maticnetwork/maticjs` SDK or call `RootChainManager.depositFor()` on Ethereum directly. Always check the `checkpointManager` sync lag before assuming PoS state is finalized on L1.

bytes.topic: EVM Compatibility — what it means, chains that run EVM bytecode, tooling reuse
bytes.title: Ethereum EVM Compatibility — Deploy Once, Run Anywhere
bytes.body:
EVM compatibility means a chain executes the same bytecode the Ethereum mainnet does. When you compile a Solidity contract with `solc`, the output is EVM bytecode — a stack-based instruction set (PUSH, MLOAD, SSTORE, CALL, etc.). Any chain that implements the EVM spec (EIP-150, EIP-155, EIP-1559 where adopted) can run that bytecode unchanged.

Chains like Polygon PoS, Arbitrum One, Optimism, Base, Avalanche C-Chain, and BNB Smart Chain all expose an EVM-compatible execution environment. Your Hardhat config only needs a new `networks` entry — same ABI, same ethers.js calls, same event signatures, same `eth_getLogs` RPC.

The catch: EVM-equivalent (like Optimism's Bedrock) ≠ EVM-compatible. Equivalent chains pass the full Ethereum test suite; compatible chains may diverge on precompiles, gas schedules, or opcode costs. Arbitrum, for instance, has a custom `ArbGas` model and added precompiles at `0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000064–0x0068`.

Always check chain-specific differences before assuming 1:1 portability — especially for assembly-heavy contracts using `SELFDESTRUCT`, `PUSH0` (EIP-3855), or `MCOPY` (EIP-5656).

bytes.topic: MEV — maximal extractable value, frontrunning, sandwich attacks, PBS
bytes.title: Ethereum MEV — Frontrunning, Sandwich Attacks, and How PBS Reshapes Block Production
bytes.body:
MEV (Maximal Extractable Value) is profit extracted by reordering, inserting, or censoring transactions within a block. On Ethereum, bots monitor the public mempool for high-value opportunities — a large Uniswap V3 swap triggers a sandwich attack: the bot frontruns with a buy on the same pool (pushing price up), lets your tx execute at a degraded rate, then immediately backtrades to pocket the difference. This is pure on-chain arbitrage at your expense.

Post-Merge, Proposer-Builder Separation (PBS) via MEV-Boost decouples block building from validation. Validators (proposers) outsource block construction to specialized builders (Flashbots, BloXroute, Titan) who compete by offering the highest `block.value`. ~90% of Ethereum blocks are now MEV-Boost blocks. Builders aggregate private orderflow and MEV bundles to maximize fees.

To avoid sandwich exposure, submit via Flashbots `eth_sendBundle`: transactions bypass the public mempool, land atomically in a target block, or revert cleanly — no partial fills, no leaked intent. Pair with tight slippage (0.5% stables, 1% volatile pairs) on Uniswap's `exactInputSingle`.

bytes.topic: Account Abstraction — ERC-4337, smart wallets, gasless transactions, paymasters
bytes.title: Ethereum Account Abstraction — ERC-4337 Paymasters and Gasless UX in Practice
bytes.body: ERC-4337 introduces a parallel mempool for UserOperations — structs that describe intent rather than raw transactions. Instead of an EOA signing a tx, a smart contract wallet (e.g. Safe, Kernel, Biconomy) validates and executes ops via the singleton EntryPoint at 0x5FF137D4b0FDCD49DcA30c7CF57E578a026d2789. The critical unlock for UX is the Paymaster: a contract that sponsors gas on behalf of users. When your UserOperation's paymasterAndData field points to a VerifyingPaymaster, the EntryPoint calls validatePaymasterUserOp, checks an off-chain signature, and debits the paymaster's deposited ETH instead of the user's wallet. This lets you onboard users with zero ETH — they sign a typed UserOperation, your backend cosigns with the paymaster key, and the Bundler (e.g. Pimlico, Alchemy's Rundler) submits to the EntryPoint. Combine with ERC-7579 modular validators and you get session keys, spend limits, and social recovery — all without forking the protocol. The EntryPoint's handleOps call is atomic: all-or-nothing across the entire batch.

bytes.topic: Bridges — locking/minting, canonical vs third-party bridges, bridge risks
bytes.title: Ethereum Bridges — Lock/Mint, Canonical vs Third-Party, and What Goes Wrong
bytes.body:
Ethereum bridges use a lock-and-mint model: ETH or ERC-20s are locked in a contract on the source chain while a synthetic representation is minted on the destination. Unwinding burns the synthetic and releases the original.

Canonical bridges — Optimism's L1StandardBridge (0x99C9fc46f92E8a1c0deC1b1747d010903E884bE1), Arbitrum's DelayedInbox, Base's native bridge — are operated by the rollup team and inherit the rollup's security model. Optimistic rollup withdrawals (L2→L1) are subject to a 7-day challenge window before finalization. L1→L2 deposits confirm in minutes.

Third-party bridges — Across Protocol, Hop, Stargate — use liquidity pools and relayers to bypass withdrawal delays, often settling in seconds. The cost: full smart-contract trust. Wormhole lost $320M (Feb 2022) and Ronin lost $625M (Mar 2022) to bridge exploits — both third-party.

Key risks to evaluate:
- Smart contract bugs in bridge or token logic
- Oracle manipulation on price-based bridges
- Liquidity fragmentation — bridged USDC ≠ native USDC until Circle's CCTP
- Source-chain reorg risk before finality is reached

Use canonical bridges when withdrawal delay is tolerable. For speed, audit third-party bridge code and check TVL concentration.

bytes.topic: Base — Coinbase's L2, OP Stack, Superchain vision
bytes.title: Ethereum on Base — OP Stack Architecture, Blob Batching, and the Superchain
bytes.body:
Base is Coinbase's Ethereum L2 built on the OP Stack — the same open-source rollup framework powering Optimism Mainnet. It runs op-geth (a patched go-ethereum) for EVM equivalence, meaning existing Solidity contracts, ABIs, and Hardhat/Foundry tooling deploy without modification. Chain ID: 8453 (mainnet), 84532 (Sepolia).

Deposits flow through the OptimismPortal contract on L1 via depositTransaction(). Withdrawals trigger a 7-day fault proof window before funds are claimable on L1 — enforced by the DisputeGameFactory under the fault proof system Base shipped in 2024.

Post-EIP-4844, Base submits transaction batches as blob transactions to the BatchInbox address, cutting data availability costs ~10x versus calldata. The L2OutputOracle has been replaced by a permissioned proposer posting output roots every ~1 hour.

The Superchain vision unifies Base, OP Mainnet, Zora, and Mode under shared fault proofs and a CrossL2Inbox for synchronous cross-chain messaging. Inter-op messages use initiateMessage() / executeMessage() — letting contracts call across OP Stack chains atomically within the cluster without bridging delays.

bytes.topic: NFT Standards — ERC-721, ERC-1155, metadata, on-chain vs off-chain
bytes.title: Ethereum NFT Standards — ERC-721 vs ERC-1155 and the Metadata Trap
bytes.body:
ERC-721 mints one token per `tokenId`, making each truly unique. ERC-1155 is a multi-token standard — one contract handles both fungible and non-fungible tokens via `balanceOf(address, id)`, enabling batch transfers with `safeBatchTransferFrom` and dramatically lower gas costs for collections.

Both standards rely on `tokenURI(uint256 tokenId)` returning a URI pointing to a JSON metadata blob. The critical distinction: most production NFTs store this JSON on IPFS or Arweave (off-chain), meaning your on-chain token is a pointer, not the asset. If the IPFS node goes dark, `image` returns a 404.

On-chain metadata — storing base64-encoded SVG or JSON directly in the contract — is immutable and censorship-resistant but expensive. Projects like Nouns DAO and Loot store everything on-chain using `abi.encodePacked` + `Base64.encode`.

For ERC-1155, the metadata URI uses a `{id}` placeholder: `https://api.example.com/token/{id}.json`, which clients replace with the hex-padded token ID. ERC-721's `tokenURI` must be overridden per-token, giving finer-grained control but no batch efficiency.

bytes.topic: Security — reentrancy, oracle manipulation, private key management
bytes.title: Ethereum Security — Reentrancy, Oracle Manipulation, and Key Management in Practice
bytes.body: Three attack vectors drain more ETH than any other. Reentrancy hits when an external contract calls back into yours before state updates — guard every external call with the checks-effects-interactions pattern or OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard. The classic DAO hack exploited exactly this: `withdraw()` sent ETH before zeroing the balance. Oracle manipulation is subtler: on-chain price feeds like a Uniswap V3 pool spot price can be moved in a single block via flash loans. Always use time-weighted average prices (TWAP) via `IUniswapV3Pool.observe()` over at least a 30-minute window, or delegate to Chainlink's `AggregatorV3Interface.latestRoundData()` with staleness checks on `updatedAt`. Private key management is the most underrated risk. Never derive keys client-side with weak entropy, never commit `.env` files, and use hardware wallets or AWS KMS for signing in production. For server-side signing, `ethers.Wallet.fromEncryptedJson()` with a secrets-manager-injected password is the minimum viable safe path.

bytes.topic: DeFi Primitives — AMMs, lending protocols, liquidations, flash loans
bytes.title: Ethereum Flash Loans — Atomic Liquidations Across Aave v3 and Uniswap v3 in One Transaction
bytes.body: Flash loans let you borrow any amount from Aave v3's pool with zero collateral, execute arbitrary logic, and repay within the same transaction — or the entire call reverts. The key interface is `IFlashLoanSimpleReceiver`: Aave calls your `executeOperation` callback mid-transaction, passing the borrowed asset, amount, and premium (currently 0.05%). The real power emerges when you chain DeFi primitives atomically. A classic pattern: borrow USDC via flash loan → call `liquidationCall` on an undercollateralized Aave position (receiving discounted WETH) → swap WETH→USDC on Uniswap v3 via `ISwapRouter.exactInputSingle` → repay flash loan + premium → pocket the spread. All within one block, no capital required. On Uniswap v3, the `sqrtPriceLimitX96` parameter lets you bound slippage precisely — critical when your profit margin is 1–3%. Aave's `ADDRESSES_PROVIDER` on mainnet is `0x2f39d218133AFaB8F2B819B1066c7E434Ad94E9e`. Always simulate with `eth_call` before broadcasting; MEV bots front-run profitable liquidations within milliseconds of mempool visibility.

bytes.topic: Finality — probabilistic vs deterministic finality, reorg depth by chain
bytes.title: Ethereum Finality — Probabilistic vs Deterministic, and Why Your Deposit Logic Matters
bytes.body:
Post-Merge Ethereum replaced probabilistic finality with cryptoeconomic determinism via Casper FFG. Under PoW, no block was ever truly irreversible — each confirmation just made a reorg exponentially costlier. Exchanges waited 35+ blocks (~7 min) as a heuristic. That's gone.

Now the beacon chain processes checkpoints every epoch (32 slots, ~6.4 min). After two epochs (~12.8 min), a checkpoint is finalized: reverting it requires slashing ≥1/3 of all staked ETH — currently ~11M ETH. That's a cryptoeconomic guarantee, not a statistical one.

Ethereum's JSON-RPC exposes three actionable block tags:
- `latest` — chain tip, can reorg (1–2 block reorgs still occur occasionally post-Merge)
- `safe` — ~2 slots behind latest, extremely unlikely to reorg
- `finalized` — irreversible, ~2 epochs behind

For deposit crediting or bridge state reads, always anchor to `finalized`. For latency-sensitive reads where 12.8 min lag is unacceptable, `safe` is acceptable with documented risk. Never build financial settlement logic on `latest`. Post-Merge mainnet data shows single-slot reorgs are rare but real; multi-slot reorgs are near-zero without a liveness failure.

bytes.topic: RPC & Nodes — full vs archive vs light nodes, public RPC risks, running your own
bytes.title: Ethereum Nodes Demystified — Full vs Archive vs Light, and Why Public RPCs Are a Risk
bytes.body:
Ethereum nodes aren't interchangeable. Full nodes (Geth, Nethermind) store current world state and a rolling chain history — Geth prunes state beyond ~128 blocks by default. Calling `eth_getBalance` at block 15,000,000 on a full node returns `missing trie node`. For historical state queries, you need an archive node, which stores every state transition since genesis — roughly 18 TB on mainnet today.

Light nodes sync only block headers and outsource state proofs to serving full nodes. Fine for lightweight wallets, not for dApps that need reliable state.

Public RPCs (Infura, Alchemy, QuickNode) hide this complexity but introduce real risks: rate limits that break under load, IP and address correlation that leaks wallet activity, and centralization — Infura's November 2020 outage took down MetaMask and most of DeFi simultaneously. For MEV-sensitive flows, your pending transactions are visible to the RPC provider before broadcast.

Snap sync makes running your own full node practical: syncs in hours, not weeks. For `eth_getLogs` over wide block ranges or replaying historical transactions, spin up an archive node or use a provider that explicitly offers archive access.

bytes.topic: Choosing an L2 — Arbitrum vs Optimism vs Base vs Polygon decision framework
bytes.title: Ethereum L2 Decision Framework — Arbitrum vs Optimism vs Base vs Polygon
bytes.body:
All four are EVM-compatible, but security models diverge sharply. Arbitrum Nitro and Optimism use fraud proofs with a 7-day challenge window before L1 finality. Base is an OP Stack fork — same security model as Optimism, Coinbase sequencer. Polygon zkEVM uses validity proofs: cryptographic finality, no challenge period. Polygon PoS is a sidechain, not a rollup — bridge security depends on its own validator set, not Ethereum consensus.

Decision axes:
- **DeFi/Liquidity**: Arbitrum — deepest GMX, Uniswap v3, Camelot liquidity.
- **Consumer/AA**: Base — native ERC-4337 infra, Coinbase distribution, Smart Wallet.
- **Superchain/L3**: Optimism — OP Stack lets you launch your own L3 inheriting L1 security.
- **ZK Finality**: Polygon zkEVM — validity proofs, no 7-day withdrawal delay for bridging at scale.
- **Gas**: All chains use EIP-4844 blobs post-Dencun — calldata costs dropped ~10×.

Gate chain-specific logic on `eth_chainId`: Arbitrum One (42161), OP Mainnet (10), Base (8453), Polygon zkEVM (1101).
